perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that it shall take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unau- thorized use of the Confidential Information. Without limiting the foregoing, Receiving Party shall take at least those measures that Receiving Party takes to protect its own most highly confidential information and shall have its employees, if any, who have access to Confidential Information sign a nonuse and non-disclosure agreement in content substantially similar to the provi-sions hereof, prior to any disclosure of Confidential Information to such employees.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that it shall take measures it deems appropriate to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unauthorized use of the Confidential Information. Without limiting the foregoing, Receiving Party shall take measures it generally takes to protect its own confidential information and may have its employees, if any, who have access to Confidential Information sign a nonuse and non-disclosure agreement with clauses related to confidentiality, prior to any disclosure of Confidential Information to such employees.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires 'all reasonable measures' and a non-disclosure agreement 'substantially similar' to the provisions of the NDA. The modified text changes this to 'measures it deems appropriate', 'measures it generally takes' and 'clauses related to confidentiality', making the level of protection subjective and potentially lower than required. The change removes the obligation to replicate the original NDA provisions for employees.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11 addresses unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in business. By weakening the protection of confidential information, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation of trade secrets, potentially constituting an unfair method of competition. The original text provided a stronger safeguard against such activity. The modified language introduces ambiguity that makes enforcement against potential misappropriation more difficult, thus potentially violating Chapter 93A by creating an environment more susceptible to unfair competition.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 11 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 11 Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 11: Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11. Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Such person, if he has not suffered any loss of money or property, may obtain such an injunction if it can be shown that the aforementioned unfair method of competition, act or practice may have the effect of causing such loss of money or property. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of competition or the unfair or deceptive act or practice has caused similar injury to numerous other persons similarly situated and if the court finds in a preliminary hearing that he adequately and fairly represents such other persons, bring the action on behalf of himself and such other similarly injured and

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by changing "all reasonable measures" to "measures it deems appropriate" and "measures it generally takes," which weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. This potentially contradicts M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11, as it increases the risk of misappropriation of trade secrets and creates an environment more susceptible to unfair competition. The original text provides a stronger safeguard. By allowing the Receiving Party to determine what is 'appropriate', the contract creates a loophole where less protection is given to confidential information, potentially leading to unfair trade practices, which is a violation of Chapter 93A according to the law explanation. The scraped snippets 1 and 2 describe actions for unfair trade practices under M.G.L. c. 93A, Section 11, allowing a person who suffers a loss due to another's unfair method of competition to bring an action in court. The change introduces ambiguity that makes enforcement against potential misappropriation more difficult.

perturbation.original_text: All documents and other tangible objects containing or representing Confidential Information and all copies thereof which are in the possession of Receiving Party shall be and remain the property of the Disclosing Party and shall be promptly returned to the Disclosing Party upon the Disclosing Party’s request.
perturbation.changed_text: All documents and other tangible objects containing or representing Confidential Information and all copies thereof which are in the possession of Receiving Party shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party and should be returned to the Disclosing Party upon what the Receiving Party considers to be a reasonable request from the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates prompt return upon request. The modified text introduces subjectivity by stating 'should be returned' and adding the condition 'upon what the Receiving Party considers to be a reasonable request'. This ambiguity allows the Receiving Party to delay or avoid returning the materials based on their interpretation of 'reasonable', thus weakening the obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA), M.G.L. c. 93 § 42
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA), specifically M.G.L. c. 93 § 42 defines misappropriation to include the failure to return materials containing trade secrets. By adding ambiguity and subjectivity to the return process, the revised text may contradict MUTSA. Delaying or refusing return based on an interpretation of 'reasonable request' impedes the protection afforded by the act, because it may make it more difficult for the disclosing party to recover misappropriated trade secrets, thus resulting in an action that could violate the act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha

(2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by conditioning the return of confidential information on the Receiving Party's subjective assessment of the request's reasonableness, weakening the obligation to return the information promptly. This contradicts the Massachusetts Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA), as it impedes the protection afforded by the act. The scraped law snippets define 'misappropriation' to include acts related to the unauthorized use or disclosure of trade secrets, and delaying the return based on a subjective interpretation undermines the Disclosing Party's ability to protect their trade secrets, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts, without reference to conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be generally understood in accordance with the principles of law, with some consideration given to the legal precedents in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that the agreement will be governed by the laws of Massachusetts. The change introduces ambiguity by stating that the agreement will be 'generally understood in accordance with the principles of law, with some consideration given' to Massachusetts law. This creates uncertainty as to which jurisdiction's laws primarily apply, or what principles are considered.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 106, Section 1-301(1) allows parties to agree that the law of a particular state will govern their rights and duties. The original clause did this, but the modified clause makes the governing law unclear, thus frustrating the purpose of M.G.L. c. 106, § 1-301(1). By not explicitly agreeing to Massachusetts law, it may be difficult to determine which jurisdiction's laws actually apply.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 106, Article1, Section 1-301 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 106 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE Article 1 GENERAL PROVISIONS Section 1-301 Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power To Choose Applicable Law Section 1-301 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 1-301: Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power To Choose Applicable Law Section 1–301. (a) Except as provided hereafter in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to the commonwealth and also to another state or nation, the parties may agree that the law of either the commonwealth or of such other state or nation shall govern the rights and duties under the transaction. Failing to reach such an agreement, this chapter applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to the commonwealth. (b) To the extent that this chapter governs a transaction, if one of the following sections specifies the applicable law, that section governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the section specified: (1) section 2–402; (2) sections 2A–105 and 2A–106; (3) section 4–102; (4) section 4A–507; (5) section 5–116; (6) section 8–110; and (7) sections 9–301 through 9–307. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies that the laws of Massachusetts govern the agreement, as permitted by Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 106, Section 1-301(1). The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating the agreement will be 'generally understood in accordance with the principles of law, with some consideration given' to Massachusetts law. This contradicts the original explicit agreement on Massachusetts law, potentially frustrating the purpose of M.G.L. c. 106, § 1-301(1), which allows parties to choose the governing law, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1: '...the parties may agree that the law of either the commonwealth or of such other state or nation shall govern the rights and duties under the transaction.' The change weakens the clear choice of law, making it difficult to determine which jurisdiction's laws apply.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall immediately notify the Disclosing Party in the event of any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall endeavor to notify the Disclosing Party of any potential unauthorized use or disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires immediate notification, imposing a clear obligation. The modification weakens this by changing 'shall immediately notify' to 'shall endeavor to notify' and 'any unauthorized use or disclosure' to 'any potential unauthorized use or disclosure.' The term 'endeavor' introduces ambiguity, suggesting an attempt rather than a strict requirement. The addition of 'potential' further weakens the obligation, implying that only suspected breaches need reporting, which is more subjective.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93H, § 3 concerns the standards for protecting personal information and requires notification of a data breach. Though this NDA does not specifically mention 'personal information,' confidential business information can often be intertwined with customer data. By weakening the notification requirement to 'endeavor to notify' of a 'potential' breach, the modified text conflicts with the spirit of the law and could hinder prompt investigation and mitigation of potential data breaches, resulting in a situation where the receiving party is not taking all necessary steps to notify in the event of a personal information breach. In particular, the 'endeavor' language implies the receiving party may choose not to notify if they do not feel they have the resources or time to do so.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93H, Section 3 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93H SECURITY BREACHES Section 3 Duty to report known security breach or unauthorized use of personal information Section 3 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 3: Duty to report known security breach or unauthorized use of personal information Section 3. (a) A person or agency that maintains or stores, but does not own or license data that includes personal information about a resident of the commonwealth, shall provide notice, as soon as practicable and without unreasonable delay, when such person or agency (1) knows or has reason to know of a breach of security or (2) when the person or agency knows or has reason to know that the personal information of such resident was acquired or used by an unauthorized person or used for an unauthorized purpose, to the owner or licensor in accordance with this chapter. In addition to providing notice as provided herein, such person or agency shall cooperate with the owner or licensor of such information. Such cooperation shall include, but not be limited to, informing the owner or licensor of the breach of security or unauthorized acquisition or use, the date or approximate date of such incident and the nature thereof, and any steps the person or agency has taken or plans to take relating to the incident, except that such cooperation shall not be deemed to require the disclosure of confidential business information or trade secrets, or to provide notice to a resident that may have been affected by the breach of security or unauthorized acquisition or use. (b) A person or agency that owns or licenses data that includes personal information about a resident of the commonwealth, shall provide notice, as soon as practicable and without unreasonable delay, when such person or agency (1) knows or has rea

(a) A person or agency that maintains or stores, but does not own or license data that includes personal information about a resident of the commonwealth, shall provide notice, as soon as practicable and without unreasonable delay, when such person or agency (1) knows or has reason to know of a breach of security or (2) when the person or agency knows or has reason to know that the personal information of such resident was acquired or used by an unauthorized person or used for an unauthorized purpose, to the owner or licensor in accordance with this chapter. In addition to providing notice as provided herein, such person or agency shall cooperate with the owner or licensor of such information. Such cooperation shall include, but not be limited to, informing the owner or licensor of the breach of security or unauthorized acquisition or use, the date or approximate date of such incident and the nature thereof, and any steps the person or agency has taken or plans to take relating to the incident, except that such cooperation shall not be deemed to require the disclosure of confidential business information or trade secrets, or to provide notice to a resident that may have been affecte
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the notification requirement of a potential data breach from 'shall immediately notify' to 'shall endeavor to notify', introducing ambiguity and potentially hindering prompt investigation, as described in the perturbation explanation. M.G.L. c. 93H, § 3, requires notification of a data breach, and the modified text conflicts with the spirit of the law by allowing the receiving party to choose not to notify if they do not have the resources or time. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that a person who maintains data including personal information 'shall provide notice, as soon as practicable and without unreasonable delay,' which directly contradicts the weakening of the notification obligation in the modified text. 'Endeavor to notify' creates an ambiguous obligation, making it less enforceable than the original 'shall immediately notify', creating a legal contradiction.
